title: URL and history state

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for URL and history state; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: MDN JavaScript Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to URL and history state
sources.claim: URL and URLSearchParams parse and serialize addresses safely relative to an explicit base instead of manual string concatenation.

canonicalExplanation.summary: URL and URLSearchParams parse and serialize addresses safely relative to an explicit base instead of manual string concatenation.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: new URL(userPath, location.origin) is checked for allowed origin and protocol before navigation. Your goal is to implement shareable filter state and handle back-forward navigation. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: DOM, events and forms; Promises, async work and HTTP; Event propagation and delegation; html navigation urls. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: URL and URLSearchParams parse and serialize addresses safely relative to an explicit base instead of manual string concatenation. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Encoding, duplicate query keys, fragments, origin checks, and dangerous schemes require deliberate validation. This draft therefore rejects: Concatenating '?' and raw user text always produces a valid encoded query.

includes: Parse and update URL search and history state without manual unsafe string construction.
Implement shareable filter state and handle back-forward navigation.
excludes: Encoding, duplicate query keys, fragments, origin checks, and dangerous schemes require deliberate validation.

outcomes: Implement shareable filter state and handle back-forward navigation.

examples.title: URL and history state: concrete case
examples.situation: new URL(userPath, location.origin) is checked for allowed origin and protocol before navigation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: new URL(userPath, location.origin) is checked for allowed origin and protocol before navigation.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use URLSearchParams for query encoding and validate protocol and origin before trusting constructed URLs.
examples.result: The result follows because Use URLSearchParams for query encoding and validate protocol and origin before trusting constructed URLs.

limitations: Human coordination does not reproduce coercion, lexical scope, microtasks, prototypes, or browser security boundaries.
